relationships.text: Resents her as unserious, expensive, and protected by J. Baroo.
Sees her as a legal nuisance trying to dilute the estate.
Needs their image work, but treats it as hired service rather than loyalty.
Sees the claim as an attack on his legitimacy and inheritance.
Knows they were close to J. Baroo, but dismisses them as theatrical noise.
Thinks the collection is an expensive distraction from the real business.
Knows planning access matters for the company, but does not grasp the private deal.
Sees the foundation as reputational machinery, not a moral project.
Believes the Director is his mentor and safest ally in succession.
Sees him as brittle, joyless, and dangerously convinced the company is his.
Questions whether the Widow is genuinely grieving or guarding status.
Sees through the brand management and dislikes being packaged.
Is suspicious, but more curious than hostile.
Suspects they mattered to J. Baroo more than the family admits.
Knows the archive or collection may contain things others want hidden.
Has little direct knowledge of the Minister’s planning interest.
Distrusts polished moral language around family money.
Does not fully trust his calm caretaker act.
Thinks he is arrogant, socially useless, and too eager to push her out.
Resents her legitimacy, ease, and emotional claim on J. Baroo.
Competes with them over who controls the public story of the marriage.
Uses the claim as leverage against the legitimate heirs.
Sees them as a rival for J. Baroo’s affection and memory.
Knows the collection holds value, but cares mainly if it affects her settlement.
Knows public respectability and political protection may be useful.
May use charitable respectability to protect her status.
May rely on him to manage estate realities after J. Baroo.
Uses him as the obvious public heir while quietly managing his damage.
Finds her hard to control because she performs indifference too well.
Needs her dignified, quiet, and predictable.
Wants to suppress, soften, or reframe the legitimacy claim.
Wants to control rumours around private letters and patronage.
Has buried rumours about the Baroo Collection before.
Coordinates political messaging around land, legacy, and reputation.
Turns charity work into clean public optics.
Works with him on crisis control and continuity messaging.
Sees him as the symbol of everything they were denied.
Wants recognition from her, not just legal acknowledgement.
Knows the Widow’s support may be strategic rather than sincere.
Sees them as the person paid to erase inconvenient people.
May believe they know the truth about parentage.
Knows little about the Antique Hunter beyond estate gossip.
Has no meaningful knowledge of the Minister’s role.
May seek legitimacy through their public moral support.
Suspects the Director treats the claim as a tool against the heirs.
Finds him crude, emotionally empty, and blind to his father’s inner life.
May see her as the only heir subtle enough to understand J. Baroo.
Knows the marriage was not what the public saw.
Knows image management is not the same as truth.
May know the parentage story, but has reasons not to say it plainly.
Shares elite patronage circles and old Baroo secrets.
Knows scandal can damage the circles the Minister needs controlled.
Supplies celebrity and glamour for philanthropic theatre.
Knows something personal that could undermine his version of J. Baroo.
Sees him as a corporate vandal who would sell what he does not understand.
Fears she will ask provenance questions the family avoided.
May know she tried to extract value from the collection.
Needs them to keep provenance questions away from the press.
May have seen records or objects tied to old private arrangements.
May know gifts or artefacts tied to their relationship with J. Baroo.
Needs permits, heritage discretion, or planning tolerance.
Resists donation or audit of the collection.
May have helped him keep collection problems quiet during company transitions.
Sees him as useful but unstable for delicate arrangements.
Sees her as a risk because she is not embedded in local arrangements.
Sees her as useful if she keeps the family respectable.
Uses them to keep public discussion disciplined.
Sees the claim as a destabilising inheritance problem.
Wants scandal contained before it affects civic arrangements.
May need collection issues kept quiet to protect land negotiations.
Uses charity and civic virtue as access.
Needs company continuity and land cooperation.
Sees him as a cheque book with poor manners.
Wants her blessing for legacy and foundation work.
Offers dignity in exchange for silence and public cooperation.
Needs their polish to make money look virtuous.
Can offer sympathy without giving real power.
Uses their glamour to make influence look like culture.
Wants collection prestige without provenance trouble.
Uses political access to keep the foundation powerful.
Needs his control to keep foundation commitments protected.
Pretends to mentor him, privately considers him unfit to lead.
Fears she may inherit records that expose the company history.
Uses her grievance to destabilise the children.
Uses them to shape the narrative after the will.
Treats the claim as a lever against the heirs.
Considers them a loose end because of what they know.
Wants collection issues managed quietly during transition.
Needs planning arrangements to survive the will.
Uses the foundation as reputation cover and influence channel.
